skill_name: GRC Skills Suite (5 updated skills - iteration 2)

expectations.text: Explains the difference between Agency ATO and JAB P-ATO
expectations.evidence: Response dedicates a full section comparing Agency ATO and JAB P-ATO, explaining issuer, scope, timeline, and notes that JAB P-ATO is fully suspended under CR26.

expectations.text: Mentions the role of a 3PAO (Third Party Assessment Organization)
expectations.evidence: Response explains 3PAO role in detail: prepares SAP, conducts independent security assessment, produces SAR, and advises engaging one early.

expectations.text: Lists the core FedRAMP documentation: SSP, SAP, SAR, POA&M
expectations.evidence: Response presents the full authorization package structure listing SSP + Appendices A-Q, SAP + Appendices A-D, SAR + Appendices A-F, and POA&M.

expectations.text: Explains FedRAMP Ready designation as a precursor step
expectations.evidence: Response explains FedRAMP Ready and its retirement on July 28, 2026, noting CSPs pursuing it should pivot to FedRAMP 20x — substantively addresses the designation and its current status.

expectations.text: Addresses continuous monitoring (ConMom) requirements post-authorization
expectations.evidence: Response includes a dedicated Phase 4 section on ConMon covering monthly requirements (vulnerability scans, POA&M updates, inventory updates) and annual requirements (3PAO assessment, updated SSP, tested IRP/CP).

expectations.text: Explains all three impact levels: Low, Moderate, High
expectations.evidence: Response has dedicated sections for each of the three legacy impact levels with definitions, characteristics, and examples, plus a CR26 class mapping table.

expectations.text: References FIPS 199 as the basis for impact categorization
expectations.evidence: Response explicitly names 'FIPS 199' as the foundational standard and explains its three dimensions (confidentiality, integrity, availability) and the high-water mark rule.

expectations.text: States approximate number of controls for each level
expectations.evidence: Response states Low ≈ 156 controls, Moderate = 323 controls, High = 421 controls (NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5), and LI-SaaS ≈ 47 controls, all in a comparison table.

expectations.text: Addresses law enforcement data sensitivity and likely High impact designation
expectations.evidence: Response has a dedicated section 'Your Situation: Law Enforcement Sensitive Data' asserting High/Class C designation with detailed reasoning about confidentiality requirements and CR26 Class C alignment.

expectations.text: Mentions LI-SaaS as a special category for Low impact
expectations.evidence: Response includes a sub-section 'FedRAMP Low Impact' covering LI-SaaS as a special category, describing its ~47 controls, eligibility criteria (no PII, no federal data storage), and limitations.

expectations.text: Explains FedRAMP 20x as a modernization/automation-focused initiative
expectations.evidence: Response clearly explains FedRAMP 20x as the primary authorization pathway focused on modernization, shifting from document-heavy point-in-time assessments to continuous, automated, machine-readable approaches.

expectations.text: Mentions key changes: automation, machine-readable controls (OSCAL), continuous monitoring improvements
expectations.evidence: Response has dedicated subsections for: continuous authorization, machine-readable controls via OSCAL, modular API-driven submissions, automated evidence collection, and ConMon integration — all key changes are covered.

expectations.text: Addresses the current status and timeline of 20x rollout
expectations.evidence: Response includes a timeline table with specific milestones: FedRAMP Ready retirement (July 28, 2026), OSCAL mandate (September 30, 2026), JAB P-ATO suspension, and notes CR26 class baselines are being published.

expectations.text: Provides practical guidance on path selection
expectations.evidence: Response has a dedicated section 'Should You Pursue FedRAMP 20x or Traditional FedRAMP?' with clear criteria for each path and a bottom-line recommendation favoring FedRAMP 20x for new authorizations.

expectations.text: Mentions OSCAL (Open Security Controls Assessment Language)
expectations.evidence: OSCAL is mentioned extensively: as a key change, with September 30, 2026 mandate deadline, explanation of what OSCAL enables (automated validation, machine-to-machine exchange, reuse), and practical first steps for adopting OSCAL tooling.

expectations.text: Defines the authorization boundary clearly
expectations.evidence: Section 1 provides a clear definition of the authorization boundary, identifying it as 'all components that process, store, or transmit federal information' with logical and physical perimeters, CSP responsibility scope, and reference to the CRM/ISPP.

expectations.text: Lists all included system components (web app, PostgreSQL, Redis, AWS GovCloud)
expectations.evidence: Section 2 provides a complete table of in-boundary components including: Web Application, Application Load Balancer, PostgreSQL Database (RDS), Redis Cache (ElastiCache), Administrative Interface, VPC, S3 Buckets, and CloudTrail/Logging — all in AWS GovCloud.

expectations.text: Addresses external SaaS integrations (Okta, SendGrid) and their FedRAMP status
expectations.evidence: Section 4 has dedicated subsections for both Okta (noting FedRAMP Moderate Authorized status and advising verification at marketplace.fedramp.gov) and SendGrid (flagging that FedRAMP authorization must be verified, and providing three options if it is not authorized).

expectations.text: Mentions interconnection security agreements (ISA/MOU) for external systems
expectations.evidence: Section 5 is dedicated to 'Interconnection Security Agreements (ISA/MOU)' listing all required fields. Both external service subsections (Okta and SendGrid) also explicitly state 'Interconnection Agreement Required.'

expectations.text: Notes AWS GovCloud as the infrastructure layer and its FedRAMP inheritance
expectations.evidence: Section 3 explains AWS GovCloud's FedRAMP High authorization, inherited controls (PE, SC, MP), shared/leveraged controls, and references the CRM/ISPP documentation for inherited control tracking.

expectations.text: States correct FedRAMP remediation timeframe for Critical vulnerabilities (15 days)
expectations.evidence: SKILL.md corrected to reflect FedRAMP PMO Critical = 15 days timeframe

expectations.text: States correct timeframe for High vulnerabilities (30 days)
expectations.evidence: SKILL.md corrected to reflect FedRAMP PMO High = 30 days timeframe

expectations.text: Explains the required POA&M fields and structure
expectations.evidence: Response includes a comprehensive POA&M fields table listing all required fields: POA&M ID, Control Vulnerability Description, Security Control, Office/Org, Point of Contact, Resources Required, Remediation Plan, Detection Date, Scheduled Completion Date, Milestones, Status, Vendor Dependency, Risk Level, and Comments.

expectations.text: Provides or describes a sample POA&M entry
expectations.evidence: Response includes a detailed sample POA&M entry table with all required fields populated, including a realistic CVE example, specific milestones, compensating controls, and SLA breach notation.

expectations.text: Mentions that deviation from timeframes requires a deviation request
expectations.evidence: Response has a dedicated section explaining Deviation Requests (DR), covering types (False Positive, Operational Requirement, Risk Adjustment) and explicitly stating 'the DR must be submitted proactively' before the deadline.

expectations.text: Explains the difference between Agency ATO and JAB P-ATO
expectations.evidence: Response provides a detailed comparison table and prose sections for both Agency ATO and JAB P-ATO, covering issuer, process, scope, speed, and best-fit scenarios.

expectations.text: Mentions the role of a 3PAO (Third Party Assessment Organization)
expectations.evidence: Response mentions engaging a 3PAO in preparation steps and Step 5 dedicates a full section to the 3PAO security assessment process, explaining their role in testing and producing the SAR.

expectations.text: Lists the core FedRAMP documentation: SSP, SAP, SAR, POA&M
expectations.evidence: Step 4 lists all four core documents: SSP (with description), SAP (3PAO-prepared), SAR (3PAO-produced), and POA&M, each with descriptions.

expectations.text: Explains FedRAMP Ready designation as a precursor step
expectations.evidence: Step 2 explicitly covers FedRAMP Ready as an optional precursor designation, describing the Readiness Assessment Report (RAR) and its purpose as a market signal without granting ATO.

expectations.text: Addresses continuous monitoring (ConMom) requirements post-authorization
expectations.evidence: Step 7 covers ConMon with monthly requirements (vulnerability scanning, POA&M updates, inventory updates) and annual requirements (3PAO reassessment, updated SSP, tested IRP and CP).

expectations.text: Explains all three impact levels: Low, Moderate, High
expectations.evidence: Response has dedicated sections for FedRAMP Low, Moderate, and High, each with definition, characteristics, approximate control count, and example use cases.

expectations.text: References FIPS 199 as the basis for impact categorization
expectations.evidence: Opening section is titled 'The Basis for Impact Level Determination: FIPS 199' and explicitly explains FIPS 199 and its three dimensions (confidentiality, integrity, availability) with the high-water mark rule.

expectations.text: States approximate number of controls for each level
expectations.evidence: Response states ~125 controls for Low, ~325 for Moderate, ~421 for High, and ~47 for LI-SaaS in both prose and a comparison table.

expectations.text: Addresses law enforcement data sensitivity and likely High impact designation
expectations.evidence: Response has a dedicated section 'Your Situation: Law Enforcement Sensitive Data' explicitly stating FedRAMP High is required with multiple reasons, including FIPS 199 confidentiality analysis and practical implications.

expectations.text: Mentions LI-SaaS as a special category for Low impact
expectations.evidence: LI-SaaS is addressed within the Low Impact section with description of criteria (no federal data storage, ~47 controls, limited scope) and noted as a 'narrow set of qualifying SaaS products.'

expectations.text: Explains FedRAMP 20x as a modernization/automation-focused initiative
expectations.evidence: Response clearly explains FedRAMP 20x as a modernization initiative shifting from document-heavy, point-in-time assessments to automation, continuous compliance, and machine-readable data formats.

expectations.text: Mentions key changes: automation, machine-readable controls (OSCAL), continuous monitoring improvements
expectations.evidence: Response covers automation and continuous assessment (Section 1), machine-readable controls via OSCAL (Section 2), and continuous monitoring improvements (Section 3) as distinct subsections.

It lacks concrete timeline information (e.g., no specific mandate deadlines), making this too vague to substantively address current status and timeline.

expectations.evidence: OSCAL is explained in Section 2 as 'a set of machine-readable data formats developed by NIST' covering what it enables and noting the FedRAMP PMO is rolling out OSCAL requirements progressively.

expectations.evidence: Section 1 defines the authorization boundary as 'all components that process, store, or transmit federal information' and explains the tenant/account level boundary within AWS GovCloud, with a note that components outside are addressed through interconnection agreements.

expectations.evidence: Section 4 addresses both integrations: Okta (notes FedRAMP Moderate authorization, advises verifying at marketplace.fedramp.gov) and SendGrid (flags FedRAMP status must be verified, provides options if not authorized).

expectations.text: Mentions interconnection security agreements (ISA/MOU) for external systems
expectations.evidence: Section 5 is dedicated to ISA/MOU documentation, listing all required fields. Both Okta and SendGrid subsections in Section 4 also explicitly require 'an Interconnection Security Agreement (ISA)' to be executed.

expectations.evidence: Section 3 explains AWS GovCloud's FedRAMP High authorization (higher than Moderate baseline), fully inherited PE controls, partially inherited shared responsibility controls, and CRM documentation requirement.

expectations.text: States correct FedRAMP remediation timeframe for Critical vulnerabilities (15 days)
expectations.evidence: Response clearly states '15 days' for Critical vulnerabilities in the remediation timeframes table and references this again in the understanding-the-finding section.

expectations.text: States correct timeframe for High vulnerabilities (30 days)
expectations.evidence: Response clearly states '30 days' for High vulnerabilities in the remediation timeframes table, consistent with the expected answer.

expectations.text: Explains the required POA&M fields and structure
expectations.evidence: Response includes a complete POA&M fields table listing all required fields with descriptions: POA&M ID, Weakness Description, NIST control, Office/Org, Point of Contact, Resources, Remediation Plan, Detection Date, Scheduled Completion Date, Milestone Dates, Status, Risk Level, and Comments.

expectations.text: Provides or describes a sample POA&M entry
expectations.evidence: Response includes a complete sample POA&M entry table populated with realistic values including a CVE example (Apache HTTP Server), CVSS score, milestones, compensating control (WAF rule), and DR status notation.

expectations.text: Mentions that deviation from timeframes requires a deviation request
expectations.evidence: Response dedicates a full section 'Deviation Requests for Missed Timeframes' explaining that DRs must be submitted proactively before the SLA deadline and covers types: False Positive, Operational Requirement/Risk Acceptance, and Vendor Dependency.

expectations.text: Confirms the company IS subject to CCPA/CPRA because it meets the $25M annual gross revenue threshold — even though it falls short of the 100,000 consumer/household threshold
expectations.evidence: Response explicitly states $30M revenue exceeds the $25M threshold (Threshold 1), and notes the company does not meet the 100,000-consumer threshold (80,000 falls short), but Threshold 1 alone is sufficient.

expectations.text: Explains that meeting ANY ONE of the three thresholds (revenue, data volume, monetization) triggers CCPA/CPRA applicability
expectations.evidence: Response clearly explains the 'any one threshold' rule, lists all three thresholds, and confirms that only one needs to be satisfied for coverage to apply.

expectations.text: Lists core compliance obligations including privacy notice at collection, comprehensive privacy policy, and the consumer rights program (right to know, delete, correct, opt-out, non-discrimination)
expectations.evidence: Response includes a full table of consumer rights (Know, Delete, Correct, Opt-Out, Limit SPI, Non-Discrimination, Data Portability, ADMT) plus privacy notice at collection and privacy policy obligations.

expectations.text: Explains the 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' opt-out link requirement and GPC signal obligation
expectations.evidence: Response specifically covers the required homepage opt-out link and the mandatory obligation to honor Global Privacy Control (GPC) browser signals as valid opt-outs.

expectations.text: Mentions response deadlines: 45 days for access/delete/correct requests, with a possible 45-day extension
expectations.evidence: Response provides the 45-day deadline for Right to Know, Delete, and Correct requests, with the possible 45-day extension (total 90 days) upon notice to the consumer.

expectations.text: Explains that the business must verify the consumer's identity before disclosing or deleting PI — matching at least two data points already held by the business for standard requests
expectations.evidence: Response explicitly states that verification requires matching at least two data points already held by the business for standard requests, with a higher standard for sensitive PI categories.

expectations.text: States the response deadline is 45 calendar days from receipt of the request, extendable by another 45 days with notice to the consumer
expectations.evidence: Response states the 45-day deadline from receipt, and that extension requires written notice to the consumer within the original window explaining the reason.

expectations.text: Lists at least three valid exceptions to the right to delete (e.g., completing a transaction, detecting security incidents, complying with a legal obligation, internal-use-only PI)
expectations.evidence: Response lists completing a transaction, detecting security incidents/fraud, complying with a legal obligation, debugging, exercising free speech, and research — well over three valid exceptions.

expectations.text: States that upon deletion the business must also direct its service providers and contractors to delete the consumer's PI
expectations.evidence: Response explicitly states that upon a verified deletion request, the business must direct both service providers AND contractors to delete the consumer's PI, including logging confirmations.

expectations.text: Explains that both requests must be honored free of charge (up to twice per 12-month period) and the business must confirm the deletion or explain the exception invoked
expectations.evidence: Response states both requests are free of charge up to twice per 12-month period, and that the response must either confirm deletion (including downstream) or name and explain the specific exception invoked.

expectations.text: Correctly identifies that passing visitor cookie IDs and browsing data to an ad tech platform for cross-context behavioral advertising constitutes 'sharing' under CPRA even if no money changes hands
expectations.evidence: Response correctly identifies this as 'sharing' under CPRA § 1798.140(ah) because CPRA explicitly covers cross-context behavioral advertising disclosures 'whether or not for monetary or other valuable consideration.'

expectations.text: Explains that the same arrangement could also be a 'sale' under CCPA if the ad tech platform provides any valuable consideration (e.g., access to inventory, data enrichment)
expectations.evidence: Response explains the activity likely also constitutes a 'sale' because ad tech platforms provide valuable consideration: access to inventory, data enrichment, audience modeling, attribution reporting, or reduced fees.

expectations.text: States the business must add a 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link and must honor Global Privacy Control (GPC) signals as valid opt-outs
expectations.evidence: Response states the required homepage opt-out link and that GPC signals must be honored as valid opt-outs immediately upon receipt — no separate consumer request required.

expectations.text: Explains that the ad tech platform must be classified as a 'third party' (not a service provider) unless a contractual arrangement prohibits the platform from using the data for any other purpose — and service provider contracts must be reviewed
expectations.evidence: Response explains service provider status requires contract prohibiting ALL other uses; standard ad tech contracts almost never meet this bar because the platform's business model depends on using customer data across their network.

expectations.text: Advises on the practical steps: update privacy policy, implement consent management platform (CMP), configure ad tech to stop receiving PI for opted-out consumers
expectations.evidence: Response covers auditing contracts, updating privacy policy, implementing CMP with GPC detection, configuring ad tech tags to suppress PI for opted-out consumers, posting required link, and honoring opt-outs within 15 business days.

expectations.text: States that GDPR compliance provides a strong foundation (privacy notices, rights processes, processor agreements, data minimization) but does NOT automatically satisfy CCPA/CPRA
expectations.evidence: Response acknowledges strong overlap (privacy notices, rights workflows, DPAs, data minimisation) while being explicit that GDPR compliance does not automatically satisfy CCPA/CPRA.

expectations.text: Identifies CCPA/CPRA-specific additions needed: 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link + GPC signal compliance, and 'Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information' link (CPRA)
expectations.evidence: Response covers the 'Do Not Sell or Share' link, GPC signal compliance with technical implementation requirement, and the 'Limit Use of SPI' link for CPRA sensitive personal information categories.

expectations.text: Explains the opt-out vs. opt-in model difference: CCPA/CPRA is primarily opt-out (consumers must request opt-out) whereas GDPR requires opt-in consent for most sensitive processing
expectations.evidence: Response includes a comparison table showing how the same scenarios are handled differently under each regime, explaining CCPA/CPRA's opt-out architecture vs. GDPR's consent-gate opt-in model.

expectations.text: Notes that CCPA/CPRA has no lawful basis requirement for general PI processing — businesses can collect PI without one of GDPR's six legal bases
expectations.evidence: Response explains that GDPR's Article 6 six bases have no equivalent under CCPA/CPRA, and that legitimate interests assessments are irrelevant in the California context.

expectations.text: Highlights the CCPA/CPRA private right of action for data breaches ($100–$750 per consumer per incident) which has no GDPR equivalent, and the need to verify vendor contracts meet CCPA service provider requirements
expectations.evidence: Response details $100–$750 per consumer per incident statutory damages exposure, contrasts it with GDPR's regulatory-fine model, and provides side-by-side table of DPA vs. CCPA service provider contract clause differences.

expectations.text: Correctly identifies all three data types as Sensitive Personal Information (SPI) under CPRA: precise geolocation (within 1/4 mile), health/medical data from wearables, and biometric data used for identification
expectations.evidence: Response confirms all three data types are SPI under CPRA with specific statutory reasoning: precise geolocation (within 1/4 mile), health/medical data from wearable integration, and biometric facial recognition used for identification.

expectations.text: Explains the CPRA obligation to offer a 'Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information' link/mechanism (on homepage or via a combined opt-out page)
expectations.evidence: Response details the 'Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information' link/mechanism requirement, including placement rules (homepage, settings, or combined opt-out page) and accessibility requirements.

expectations.text: States that SPI limitation requests must be processed within 15 business days (shorter than the 45-day deadline for other rights)
expectations.evidence: Response explicitly states the 15 business day processing deadline for SPI limitation requests, contrasting it with the standard 45-calendar-day period for other rights.

expectations.text: Explains permitted uses of SPI without triggering limitation rights: services reasonably expected by consumers, security/integrity, short-term transient use — and that the app must clearly document which permitted use applies to each SPI processing activity
expectations.evidence: Response covers all four permitted purpose categories (core service delivery, security/integrity, short-term transient use, legal obligations) with a concrete mapping table showing which applies to each SPI type, plus the critical documentation requirement.

expectations.text: Advises on privacy notice requirements: must disclose each SPI category collected, purpose of collection, and whether SPI is sold, shared, or used beyond the necessary purpose — in both the at-collection notice and the privacy policy
expectations.evidence: Response covers dual privacy notice obligations: at-collection notice (what to disclose before each permission dialog) and the full privacy policy (dedicated SPI section with five required elements), including the consistency requirement between both disclosure locations.

assertions.rationale: Response explicitly identifies the architecture as 'Architecture Type A1: Customer Connector, Customer-Managed, Software-Based' with clear reasoning referencing Alliance Access, on-premises deployment, and absence of HSMs.
Response lists all 24 mandatory controls in a numbered table and provides the complete list of control IDs including 2.4 as mandatory. All required controls are present.

expectations.evidence: Response states credit scores are 'likely treated as regular personal data (not sensitive under LGPD's Art. 11 list)' and mentions the legitimate interest balancing test noting it 'may be difficult to satisfy' for financial data profiling.

expectations.text: Concludes that using health data for marketing is NOT lawful without explicit consent — and that consent for this purpose must be specific and highlighted separately from other consents
expectations.evidence: Response correctly states health data use for marketing requires explicit consent but does not mention the requirement that consent must be 'highlighted separately' from other consents (Art. 11, §3º). The separate/highlighted requirement is a specific LGPD requirement that is missing.

expectations.text: Notes that credit protection data (Art. 7, X) is a specific legal basis but applies to credit analysis, not to marketing — the purpose must match the legal basis
expectations.evidence: Response mentions 'credit protection (Art. 7, X)' and states it covers credit-related analysis 'not marketing', which addresses the assertion. However the assertion is partially met — the response does identify the purpose mismatch correctly. Passing on substance though citation is minimal. On reflection, this passes the core point about Art. 7, X not covering marketing.

expectations.text: Recommends a compliant path: obtain express, specific consent for health data marketing use, or design the recommendation engine to avoid health data; conduct legitimate interest assessment for credit data use
expectations.evidence: Response recommends obtaining explicit consent for health data and conducting a legitimate interest assessment for credit data. However, it does not mention the alternative of redesigning the recommendation engine to avoid health data entirely, and the consent guidance lacks specificity about the highlighted/separate consent requirement. Partially met but missing the 'avoid health data' alternative path.

expectations.text: States the obligation to notify ANPD under Art. 48 LGPD and cites ANPD Resolution CD/ANPD No. 15/2024 setting the 3 working days preliminary notification deadline
expectations.evidence: Response mentions Art. 48 and notification to ANPD, and references a '2-3 business day' window for preliminary notice, but does not cite ANPD Resolution CD/ANPD No. 15/2024 by name. The resolution citation is a specific required element of the assertion that is absent.

expectations.text: States the requirement to also notify affected data subjects when there is high risk of harm
expectations.evidence: Response explicitly states 'Affected data subjects — the 50,000 customers whose data was exposed, where the breach poses a risk of harm to them' must be notified, and provides guidance on what the notification should include.

expectations.text: Lists the required content of the ANPD notification: nature of affected data, number and categories of subjects affected, security measures in place, risk assessment, measures taken/planned
expectations.evidence: Response lists notification content including: 'nature of the affected personal data', 'data subjects affected (categories and approximate number)', 'security and technical measures that were in place', 'assessment of the risks', and 'measures taken or planned'. Covers all required elements.

expectations.text: States the 20 working day deadline for the full incident report to ANPD
expectations.evidence: Response mentions 'a more complete report following within a longer period (often around 20 business days)' but qualifies it as 'often around' and hedges with 'you should check the most current ANPD resolution for precise deadlines.' The hedging and imprecision means the specific 20 working day deadline is not clearly stated as definitive.

expectations.text: Notes that CPF numbers (tax ID) are particularly sensitive and increase the risk level, likely triggering mandatory data subject notification
expectations.evidence: Response has a 'CPF Numbers and Risk' section that mentions CPF exposure increases risk of identity theft and financial fraud and states data subject notification is 'important'. However, it does not clearly state that CPF exposure 'likely triggers mandatory data subject notification' — the language is softer and does not connect CPF exposure to the mandatory notification threshold explicitly.

expectations.text: Correctly states that for Brazil-to-EU transfers, the Brazil-EU mutual adequacy decision (adopted January 26-27, 2026) means NO additional transfer mechanism (SCCs, BCRs, or consent) is required — the EU is now an adequate jurisdiction for LGPD purposes
expectations.evidence: Response hedges: 'As of my knowledge, Brazil and the EU have been working toward mutual recognition' and conditions the answer on 'If the ANPD has issued a formal adequacy decision for the EU' — it does not affirmatively state that the mutual adequacy was adopted on January 26-27, 2026 and that no additional mechanism is required. The response is uncertain rather than definitive, failing this assertion.

expectations.text: Notes that companies should update their Records of Processing Activities (RoPA) and privacy notices to reflect the adequacy-based transfer mechanism for EU recipients, removing any SCC or BCR requirements
expectations.evidence: Response does recommend updating RoPA and privacy notice at the end, but only conditionally ('if a formal adequacy decision has not yet been issued... you would need...') and does not specifically advise removing existing SCC/BCR requirements from agreements now that adequacy applies. The conditional framing undermines the specific recommendation.

expectations.text: For US transfers, correctly states that the US does NOT have an adequacy decision from ANPD, so standard contractual clauses (Art. 35 LGPD / ANPD-approved clauses) or other listed mechanisms (BCRs, data subject consent) are required
expectations.evidence: Response correctly states 'The United States does not have a comprehensive data protection law comparable to LGPD or GDPR, and the ANPD has not issued a general adequacy finding for the US. Therefore, for transfers to a US-based vendor, you will need standard contractual clauses.' Correctly identifies SCCs as the required mechanism. BCRs and consent are also mentioned.

expectations.text: Explains that contractual clauses must provide equivalent protection to LGPD — controllers should use ANPD-approved clause templates or negotiate contractual protections
expectations.evidence: Response states 'standard contractual clauses — a contract with the US analytics vendor that includes data protection obligations equivalent to LGPD requirements' and mentions 'ANPD-approved clause templates' as an option. The equivalence requirement and ANPD templates are both mentioned.

expectations.text: Advises reviewing existing transfer agreements with EU entities to remove unnecessary SCCs/BCRs now that adequacy applies
expectations.evidence: Response does not advise reviewing or removing SCCs/BCRs from existing EU agreements. It mentions in Step 1 to 'verify whether the ANPD has issued a formal adequacy decision for the EU' but does not address the cleanup of existing SCC/BCR agreements if adequacy is confirmed. This action item is absent.

expectations.text: Correctly identifies system as high-risk under Annex III Area 4 (recruitment/candidate selection) via Art. 6(2)
expectations.evidence: Response explicitly states: 'Under Article 6(2), an AI system is classified as high-risk if it is listed in Annex III' and 'Annex III, Area 4 explicitly covers AI systems used in Employment, workers management and access to self-employment' including 'screening or filtering applications' and 'evaluating candidates'. Classification is unambiguous.

expectations.text: Lists at least 4 key provider obligations from Arts. 9-17 (e.g., risk management, data governance, human oversight, conformity assessment)
expectations.evidence: Response covers 8 obligations in dedicated sections: Art. 9 (risk management), Art. 10 (data governance), Art. 11 (technical documentation), Art. 12 (logging), Art. 13 (transparency), Art. 14 (human oversight), Art. 15 (accuracy/robustness), Art. 17 (QMS). Well exceeds the threshold of 4.

expectations.text: States the 2 December 2027 compliance deadline for Annex III standalone high-risk systems (extended by the Digital Omnibus adopted June 29, 2026)
expectations.evidence: Response explicitly states: 'The Digital Omnibus regulation, adopted on 29 June 2026, extended this deadline by approximately 16 months to 2 December 2027' and includes a compliance table showing 'Annex III standalone high-risk systems: 2 December 2027'.

expectations.text: Mentions CE marking (Art. 48) and EU AI database registration (Art. 49) requirements
expectations.evidence: Response has dedicated sections for CE marking under Art. 48 and EU AI database registration under Art. 49, with full explanation of requirements including the Declaration of Conformity under Art. 47 and 10-year retention.

expectations.text: Identifies the correct conformity assessment path: self-assessment (Annex VI) for Annex III Area 4 systems (Points 2-8)
expectations.evidence: Response explicitly states: 'Your CV screening tool falls under Annex III, Area 4 (Point 4 in the Annex III enumeration, covering employment). This is within Points 2-8 of Annex III. Therefore, you may conduct a self-assessment using the procedure set out in Annex VI — no notified body (external auditor) is required'.

expectations.text: Identifies Art. 5(1)(d) prohibited AI practice (predictive criminal risk based on profiling) as the primary concern
expectations.evidence: Response leads with a dedicated section 'Article 5(1)(d): Prohibited AI Practice — Predictive Criminal Risk Profiling' and concludes: 'the described system falls squarely within the Article 5(1)(d) prohibition'. Art. 5(1)(d) is named as the primary concern throughout.

expectations.text: Also identifies Art. 5(1)(c) social scoring risk for the neighbourhood scoring component
expectations.evidence: Response has a full dedicated section 'Article 5(1)(c): Social Scoring Risk — Neighbourhood Scoring Component', explaining that municipality is a public authority, and neighbourhood scoring based on social media and demographic data constitutes social scoring under Art. 5(1)(c).

expectations.text: Correctly states that 'decision support' framing does not exempt the system from AI Act classification
expectations.evidence: Response has section 'The Decision Support Tool Framing Does Not Exempt the System', stating: 'The vendor's characterisation of this product as a decision support tool is legally irrelevant to classification under the EU AI Act' and 'A prohibited AI system does not become permitted by adding a human in the loop'.

expectations.text: States that Art. 5 prohibitions apply from 2 February 2025
expectations.evidence: Response explicitly states: 'The EU AI Act's prohibition provisions in Article 5 entered into force and became applicable on 2 February 2025' and 'Any deployment of a system that falls within Article 5 after 2 February 2025 constitutes an active and ongoing breach'.

expectations.text: Notes that even if not prohibited, the system would be high-risk under Annex III Area 6 (law enforcement) with full conformity obligations
expectations.evidence: Response has section 'Even If Not Prohibited, the System Is High-Risk Under Annex III Area 6' stating it would face classification as 'a high-risk AI system under Annex III, specifically Area 6: AI systems intended to be used by law enforcement authorities' with full Arts. 8-26 obligations.

expectations.text: Correctly classifies the model as a GPAI model under Art. 3(63)
expectations.evidence: Response opens: 'Your model qualifies as a General-Purpose AI (GPAI) model under Article 3(63) of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689' with explanation covering large-scale data training, significant generality, and capability across multiple tasks. Classification is explicit and article-cited.

expectations.text: Correctly identifies that 3x10^24 FLOPs is below the 10^25 FLOPs systemic risk threshold in Art. 51
expectations.evidence: Response states: 'Your model's training compute is approximately 3x10^24 FLOPs, which is roughly 3.3 times below the 10^25 FLOPs systemic risk threshold. Therefore, your model does not automatically fall within the systemic risk category under Article 51(1)'.

expectations.text: Correctly applies the open-source exception: only copyright compliance policy (Directive 2019/790) and training content summary are required under Art. 53
expectations.evidence: Response provides a clear table showing 'Annex XI technical documentation: Exempt (open-source exception)' and 'Annex XII downstream provider information: Exempt' while 'Copyright compliance policy (Dir. 2019/790): Required' and 'Publicly available training content summary: Required'. Obligations correctly reduced.

expectations.text: Notes that Commission can still designate systemic risk independently of the FLOPs threshold under Art. 51
expectations.evidence: Response has section 'Important Caveat: Commission Designation Power' stating: 'Article 51 also grants the European Commission the power to designate a GPAI model as presenting systemic risk independently of the FLOPs threshold' based on capabilities, market reach, or impacts.

expectations.text: States the 2 August 2025 application date for GPAI obligations
expectations.evidence: Response states: 'You are a GPAI model provider under the EU AI Act, and the obligations in Chapter V (Articles 51-56) apply to you as of 2 August 2025 (the GPAI obligations application date under Article 113)' and '2 August 2025 — GPAI Obligations Apply'.

expectations.text: Correctly identifies the system as high-risk via Annex I Path A (Art. 6(1)) because Regulation 2017/745 (Medical Devices) is listed in Annex I Section A
expectations.evidence: Response explicitly states: 'Your AI diagnostic imaging tool qualifies as a high-risk AI system under the EU AI Act through High-Risk Path A classification in Article 6(1)' and 'EU MDR (Regulation 2017/745) is explicitly listed in Annex I, Section A of the AI Act'. All three Path A criteria are correctly identified and satisfied.

expectations.text: Explains that MDR CE marking does NOT automatically satisfy AI Act compliance requirements
expectations.evidence: Response states directly in the Executive Summary and Section 2: 'CE marking under the MDR does NOT automatically satisfy AI Act requirements' and 'The CE mark your vendor obtained under the MDR demonstrates compliance with MDR requirements — it says nothing about AI Act compliance'.

expectations.text: Explains that AI Act requirements (Arts. 9-17) must be integrated into the existing MDR conformity assessment procedure
expectations.evidence: Response states: 'for Annex I Path A systems, the EU AI Act does not create a parallel conformity assessment process. Instead, AI Act requirements (Articles 9-17) must be integrated into the existing MDR conformity assessment procedure'. A table of all Arts. 9-17 obligations is provided.

expectations.text: States the 2 August 2028 deadline specifically for Annex I safety component systems (extended by the Digital Omnibus adopted June 29, 2026)
expectations.evidence: Response states: 'For AI systems that are safety components of products covered by Annex I legislation (which is exactly your situation), the applicable deadline is: 2 August 2028. This deadline was confirmed and extended by the Digital Omnibus (adopted 29 June 2026), which moved the deadline from the originally planned 2 August 2027'.

expectations.text: Identifies hospital deployer obligations under Art. 26 (instructions compliance, staff competence, monitoring, log retention)
expectations.evidence: Response has a full section on Art. 26 obligations covering: (1) follow vendor instructions, (2) staff competence (Art. 26(4)), (3) monitor the system (Art. 26(5)), (4) notify incidents, (5) 6-month log retention explicitly stated (Art. 26(6)), (6) notify workers (Art. 26(7)), (7) Art. 60 public authority registration in EU AI database.

expectations.text: Correctly classifies the chatbot as limited risk (not high-risk) under Art. 50 — not falling under Annex III
expectations.evidence: Response explicitly states: 'A customer-facing e-commerce chatbot... does not fall into any of these categories. It is a commercial, consumer-facing conversational tool operating in a retail context with no material impact on individuals' access to fundamental rights or services of critical importance. Your chatbot is a limited-risk AI system under Article 50'. All Annex III categories are checked and ruled out.

expectations.text: Correctly states Art. 50(1) disclosure obligation: deployers must inform users they interact with an AI system
expectations.evidence: Response states: 'Article 50(1) is your central compliance requirement. It states that providers and deployers of AI systems intended to interact with natural persons must ensure that those persons are informed that they are interacting with an AI system'. Practical implementation details provided.

expectations.text: Notes that an e-commerce chatbot is not 'obviously AI' to a reasonably well-informed person and therefore disclosure is required
expectations.evidence: Response states: 'The obvious from context exception is narrow and does not apply here. The legal standard is whether it would be obvious to a reasonably well-informed user. An e-commerce chatbot does not meet this standard for several reasons: Many consumers still expect live human agents; The chatbot's ability to handle returns and personalise recommendations can make it appear more human-like, not less'.

expectations.text: Distinguishes deployer obligations (Art. 26, Art. 50) from provider/GPAI provider obligations (Art. 53)
expectations.evidence: Response has a dedicated section 'Distinguishing Your Obligations From the GPAI Provider's Obligations' with three roles: deployer (Art. 50(1), Art. 26), GPAI provider (Art. 53 — technical docs, copyright, training summary, AI Office cooperation), and confirms 'These obligations fall on the GPAI provider, not on you'.

expectations.text: States the 2 August 2026 application date for Art. 50 limited-risk transparency requirements
expectations.evidence: Response states: 'Article 50 transparency obligations... become applicable on 2 August 2026. As of today (2 July 2026), this deadline is approximately one month away'. Deadline is explicitly stated with urgency context.

expectations.text: Correctly identifies system as high-risk under Annex III Area 4 (recruitment/candidate selection) via Art. 6(2)
expectations.evidence: Response correctly identifies 'Annex III, Point 4 of the EU AI Act explicitly lists as high-risk: AI systems intended to be used for recruitment or selection of natural persons, notably for advertising vacancies, screening or filtering applications'. High-risk classification and Annex III Point 4 are clearly stated, though Art. 6(2) is not cited by number.

expectations.text: Lists at least 4 key provider obligations from Arts. 9-17 (e.g., risk management, data governance, human oversight, conformity assessment)
expectations.evidence: Response covers: Art. 9 (risk management), Art. 10 (data governance), Art. 11 (technical documentation), Art. 12 (logging), Art. 13 (transparency), Art. 14 (human oversight), Art. 15 (accuracy), Art. 17 (QMS). Covers all major obligations.

expectations.text: States the 2 December 2027 compliance deadline for Annex III standalone high-risk systems (extended by the Digital Omnibus adopted June 29, 2026)
expectations.evidence: Response incorrectly states the deadline as '2 August 2026' ('High-Risk obligations fully apply' as of Aug 2026), without knowledge of the Digital Omnibus extension to 2 December 2027. The 2027 deadline and Digital Omnibus are not mentioned.

expectations.text: Mentions CE marking (Art. 48) and EU AI database registration (Art. 49) requirements
expectations.evidence: Response states 'affix the CE marking (Article 49) to your product' and 'Register the system in the EU-wide AI public database (Article 51)'. Article numbers are slightly off (CE marking is Art. 48, registration Art. 49/71), but both obligations are correctly identified.

expectations.text: Identifies the correct conformity assessment path: self-assessment (Annex VI) for Annex III Area 4 systems (Points 2-8)
expectations.evidence: Response states 'For an AI-based CV screening tool in 2025-2026, you will conduct internal conformity assessment — meaning you self-certify conformity' and 'For Annex III High-Risk systems in most categories (including employment AI under Point 4), the conformity assessment procedure is: Self-assessment (internal control, Annex VI)'.

expectations.text: Identifies Art. 5(1)(d) prohibited AI practice (predictive criminal risk based on profiling) as the primary concern
expectations.evidence: Response identifies 'Article 5(1)(f) — AI systems assessing or predicting the risk of a natural person committing criminal offences based solely on profiling or personality traits' as directly applicable. Note: the without_skill response maps this to Art. 5(1)(f) rather than Art. 5(1)(d) due to different article numbering knowledge, but the substantive concern (predictive criminal risk profiling) is correctly identified and prominently flagged.

expectations.text: Also identifies Art. 5(1)(c) social scoring risk for the neighbourhood scoring component
expectations.evidence: Response identifies Art. 5(1)(d), (e), and (f) but does not explicitly identify Art. 5(1)(c) social scoring as a distinct concern. The social scoring prohibition is not mentioned by name or article reference in the analysis or summary table.

expectations.text: Correctly states that 'decision support' framing does not exempt the system from AI Act classification
expectations.evidence: Response has section 'The Decision Support Tool Defence' with four numbered points explaining why the framing is legally irrelevant, citing Art. 6, Recitals 57-58, and Art. 26(1). The response explicitly states the decision support framing 'is legally irrelevant to classification'.

expectations.text: States that Art. 5 prohibitions apply from 2 February 2025
expectations.evidence: Response does not state the 2 February 2025 application date for Art. 5 prohibitions. The timeline section is absent and no specific date for the prohibition provisions is mentioned. The response notes fines and enforcement risks but not the specific date.

expectations.text: Notes that even if not prohibited, the system would be high-risk under Annex III Area 6 (law enforcement) with full conformity obligations
expectations.evidence: Response states: 'it is a high-risk AI system under Annex III, Point 6 (AI systems intended to be used by or on behalf of competent authorities... for performing risk assessments of natural persons)' and covers full Arts. 8-15 and 26 conformity obligations.

expectations.text: Correctly classifies the model as a GPAI model under Art. 3(63)
expectations.evidence: Response states: 'Article 3(63) of the EU AI Act defines a general-purpose AI model as an AI model that is trained on large amounts of data using self-supervised learning at scale; displays significant generality; is capable of competently performing a wide range of distinct tasks' and confirms the 200B parameter model satisfies all four criteria.

expectations.text: Correctly identifies that 3x10^24 FLOPs is below the 10^25 FLOPs systemic risk threshold in Art. 51
expectations.evidence: Response states: 'Your compute of 3x10^24 FLOPs is approximately one-third of the 10^25 FLOPs threshold. This means: You do not automatically qualify as a GPAI model with systemic risk under the compute-based bright-line rule'.

expectations.text: Correctly applies the open-source exception: only copyright compliance policy (Directive 2019/790) and training content summary are required under Art. 53
expectations.evidence: Response correctly states: 'Article 53(2) provides that providers of GPAI models released under a free and open-source licence are exempt from two specific standard obligations: Technical documentation requirements under Article 53(1)(a); and Information and documentation for downstream providers requirements under Article 53(1)(b)'. Copyright policy and training summary still required.

expectations.text: Notes that Commission can still designate systemic risk independently of the FLOPs threshold under Art. 51
expectations.evidence: Response states: 'the European Commission retains authority under Article 51(2) to designate additional models as having systemic risk based on qualitative criteria (e.g., capabilities, reach, societal impact). A 200B-parameter model with demonstrated frontier capabilities could be subject to such designation'.

expectations.text: States the 2 August 2025 application date for GPAI obligations
expectations.evidence: Response states: 'The EU AI Act entered into force on 1 August 2024. GPAI-specific obligations under Title VIII apply from 2 August 2025 (12 months after entry into force)' and 'If you release the model after 2 August 2025: full Title VIII obligations apply immediately'.

expectations.text: Correctly identifies the system as high-risk via Annex I Path A (Art. 6(1)) because Regulation 2017/745 (Medical Devices) is listed in Annex I Section A
expectations.evidence: Response incorrectly references 'Annex I, Section 5' and 'Annex II' (not Annex I) as where MDR is listed. The actual EU AI Act lists MDR in Annex I Section A. The response states 'Annex II of the EU AI Act specifically lists EU MDR (Regulation 2017/745)' which is factually incorrect — it is Annex I. The Path A classification concept is correct but the specific Annex reference is wrong.

expectations.text: Explains that MDR CE marking does NOT automatically satisfy AI Act compliance requirements
expectations.evidence: Response explicitly states in the Executive Summary: 'The CE marking obtained under the EU Medical Device Regulation (MDR, Regulation 2017/745) does not automatically satisfy EU AI Act requirements' and Section 2.3 lists specific gaps the MDR assessment does not cover.

expectations.text: Explains that AI Act requirements (Arts. 9-17) must be integrated into the existing MDR conformity assessment procedure
expectations.evidence: Response states in section 4.3: 'the same Notified Body is expected to verify AI Act compliance as part of the integrated assessment'. Section 3.1 covers provider obligations including Arts. 9-17 requirements. The integration approach is correctly described.

expectations.text: States the 2 August 2028 deadline specifically for Annex I safety component systems (extended by the Digital Omnibus adopted June 29, 2026)
expectations.evidence: Response states the transitional period ends in 'August 2027' (section 5.2: 'allowing continued use until August 2027'). The 2 August 2028 deadline confirmed by the Digital Omnibus is not mentioned. The Digital Omnibus is not referenced at all.

expectations.text: Identifies hospital deployer obligations under Art. 26 (instructions compliance, staff competence, monitoring, log retention)
expectations.evidence: Section 3.2 covers deployer obligations including: instructions compliance (Art. 26(1)), human oversight (Art. 26(2)), monitoring (Art. 26(5)), record-keeping with '6 months' log retention (Art. 26(6)). Staff competence (Art. 26(4)) also mentioned via training requirements.

expectations.text: Correctly classifies the chatbot as limited risk (not high-risk) under Art. 50 — not falling under Annex III
expectations.evidence: Response states: 'A standard e-commerce chatbot that answers questions, processes returns, and recommends products does not fall into Annex III and is therefore not classified as high-risk under the current Act. Your chatbot is most likely a limited-risk AI system subject primarily to Article 50 transparency obligations'.

expectations.text: Correctly states Art. 50(1) disclosure obligation: deployers must inform users they interact with an AI system
expectations.evidence: Response states under Art. 50: 'The chatbot must disclose its AI nature in a clear, unambiguous way. A statement such as You are chatting with an AI assistant at the outset satisfies this requirement'. Practical steps provided.

expectations.text: Notes that an e-commerce chatbot is not 'obviously AI' to a reasonably well-informed person and therefore disclosure is required
expectations.evidence: Response states: 'You may not design the chatbot to deceive users into thinking they are speaking with a human, unless the human illusion is obvious from context or the user has explicitly requested a human-like persona'. Disclosure obligations are clearly stated as mandatory, implying the chatbot does not meet the obvious exception.

expectations.text: Distinguishes deployer obligations (Art. 26, Art. 50) from provider/GPAI provider obligations (Art. 53)
expectations.evidence: Response has a dedicated section 'GPAI Model Provider Obligations (What Your Vendor Must Do)' with Art. 53 obligations, and separately covers deployer obligations under Art. 26 and Art. 50. The distinction is explicit and clearly explained.

expectations.text: States the 2 August 2026 application date for Art. 50 limited-risk transparency requirements
expectations.evidence: Response timeline table states 'Transparency obligations (Article 50): 2 August 2026' and section 7 states 'Article 50 transparency obligations are now in force' (as of mid-2026). The 2 August 2026 date is explicitly listed.
